region_of_origin: China; widely used in Singapore and Southeast Asia

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical side serving of soaked black fungus is approximately 80 g, equivalent to a small bowl of rehydrated pieces served as a vegetable side dish or stir-fry component.

nutrient_source.title: Soaked Black Fungus — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F14030007 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken from this source.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mushrooms and Fungi — Analogous Estimate Based on Low-Carbohydrate Fungal Foods
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for soaked black fungus (Auricularia auricula-judae) is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at approximately 15 based on the analogous low-carbohydrate mushroom category (GI range 10–20 for fungi with high beta-glucan and dietary fibre content). Glycemic load per 80 g serving = (15 × 6.0 × 0.80) / 100 = 0.72, rounded to 0.7. This is consistent with the very low carbohydrate content (6 g/100 g) and high dietary fibre (2.6 g/100 g) of this food.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely low sodium (9 mg/100 g) with beneficial potassium (52 mg/100 g) and calcium (34 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Soaked black fungus is highly suitable for individuals with hypertension due to its extremely low sodium content of 9 mg/100 g; however, patients should avoid adding soy sauce, oyster sauce, or salt-based seasonings during preparation, as these would substantially increase sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (6 g/100 g), low estimated GI (~15), and moderate dietary fibre (2.6 g/100 g) supporting glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Soaked black fungus has a very low estimated glycaemic index of approximately 15 and a glycaemic load of only 0.7 per 80 g serving, making it an excellent vegetable choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes; its beta-glucan content may further attenuate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre, and notable iron content (5.5 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Soaked black fungus is cardioprotective due to its negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and dietary fibre content of 2.6 g/100 g; its iron content of 5.5 mg/100 g supports haematopoiesis, and its beta-glucan polysaccharides have been associated with lipid-lowering effects in peer-reviewed literature.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beta-glucan-rich, low saturated fat, no trans fat, dietary fibre supporting gut-immune regulation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Black fungus is a beta-glucan-rich food with immune-modulating properties and low saturated fat content, making it suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; avoid pairing with high-sodium or deep-fried accompaniments that may offset anti-inflammatory benefits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Soaked black fungus (泡发木耳; Auricularia auricula-judae) is a rehydrated edible fungus widely used in Chinese and Singaporean cuisine. It is prepared by soaking dried black fungus pieces in water for 20–30 minutes until they expand into dark brown, gelatinous, ear-shaped pieces with a soft, chewy, and slightly crunchy texture and a mild earthy flavour. Per 100 g, soaked black fungus provides only 27 kcal, 1.5 g protein, 0.2 g fat, 6.0 g carbohydrate, 2.6 g dietary fibre, and 9 mg sodium, making it one of the lowest-calorie and lowest-sodium vegetables available. Its iron content of 5.5 mg/100 g is notably high for a plant food, supporting haematopoiesis. The food is rich in beta-glucan polysaccharides, which have been associated with immune modulation and lipid-lowering effects. It is suitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease. Commonly served as a stir-fried vegetable side dish, in soups, cold salads, or as a component of braised dishes. It is vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, halal, and low-calorie. Known in Mandarin as mù ěr or hēi mù ěr, and colloquially as wood ear mushroom or cloud ear fungus.
